Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

## LEARNING TO IGNORE: SINGLE SOURCE DOMAIN GENERALIZATION VIA ORACLE REGULARIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Machine learning frequently suffers from the discrepancy in data distribution, commonly known as domain shift. Single-source Domain Generalization (sDG) is a task designed to simulate domain shift artificially, in order to train a model that can generalize well to multiple unseen target domains from a single source domain. A popular approach is to learn robustness via the alignment of augmented samples. However, prior works frequently overlooked what is learned from such alignment. In this paper, we study the effectiveness of augmentation-based sDG methods by analyzing the data generating process. We highlight issues in using augmentation for OOD generalization, namely, the distinction between domain invariance and augmentation invariance. To alleviate these issues, we introduce a novel regularization method that leverages pretrained models to guide the learning process via a feature-level regularization of mutual information, which we name PROF (Progressive mutual information Regularization for Online distillation of Frozen oracles). PROF can be applied to conventional augmentation-based methods to moderate the stochasticity of models repeatedly trained on augmented data. We show that PROF stabilizes the learning process for sDG.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Distribution shift is prevalent in many machine learning settings. The term is often referred to as _domain shift_ , where a domain is understood as the joint probability distribution from which samples are drawn. An important aspect of domain shift is that it severely hinders the generalizability of trained models (Kurakin et al., 2018). The issue is easily observable when a model trained in a source domain suffers in a target domain that is inconsistent with the source. Domain Generalization (DG) is a task specifically devised to test a model’s robustness under domain shift, where the model is given multiple labeled datasets at training time (Gulrajani & Lopez-Paz, 2021). Single-source Domain Generalization (sDG) is a variant of DG, where only a single source domain is provided at train time. The absence of additional source domains makes sDG challenging, mainly because conventional DG methods that leverage multiple domains cannot be easily adopted. To overcome such barriers, prior works on sDG often utilize data augmentation to generate unseen domains (Volpi et al., 2018) and learn domain-invariant features through an alignment of the generated domains using self-supervised contrastive loss (Oord et al., 2018) (hereinafter contrastive loss).

However, there is a relative void in the discussion on what is learned through the alignment of augmented samples. In this paper, we analyze the effectiveness of augmentation-based sDG approaches from a novel perspective of style-content disentanglement. Style-Content (S-C) disentanglement aims to identify a partitioned latent space, namely style, and content (Ren et al., 2021; Hyvarinen & Morioka, 2016). While the definitions of style and content vary across settings, here we define content as latent features that are invariant across augmentations (i.e. augment-invariant), while style is the latent feature subpart that changes with the augmentation. Recently, Von K¨ugelgen et al. (2021) studied an interesting connection between S-C disentanglement and data augmentation, demonstrating that contrastive learning provably learns to retrieve the augment-invariant features under some assumptions. We connect the discovery to the sDG literature to analyze the effectiveness of retrieving domain-invariant information from augmented data. We examine the problem from a causal standpoint by illustrating it via a causal graph (Pearl, 2009).

We state our contributions as follows. (1) We analyze the single source domain generalization task through the lens of S-C disentanglement and highlight the difficulties of learning domain-invariant information from augmentation-based sDG methods. (2) We empirically show that augmentationbased sDG methods display large fluctuations in OOD performance across various datasets (3) To mitigate the issues brought by the aforementioned obstacles, we introduce a novel regularization method PROF for sDG. (4) We further devise a novel alignment objective MDAR (Multi-Domain Alignment with Redundancy reduction) that serves as a strong sDG baseline.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

Learning domain agnostic models from limited source domains is a longstanding area of investigation. In this section, we revisit related works on S-C disentanglement and domain generalization.

**Style-Content Disentanglement** S-C disentanglement seeks to separate the aggregated latent variable into two parts, denoted as style and content. While the term style and content originated from the style transfer literature (Mathieu et al., 2016; Szab´o et al., 2017), recent works try to push the idea further using concepts of causal inference (Pearl, 2009; Peters et al., 2017) and Independent Component Analysis (ICA) (Locatello et al., 2018; Gresele et al., 2021; Reizinger et al., 2022). Notably, disentanglement is used to elucidate the underlying mechanism of data augmentation (Von K¨ugelgen et al., 2021; Ilse et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2022; Mitrovic et al., 2021).

**Domain Generalization** In the multi-source domain generalization field, disentanglement of domain-invariant features has shown great success in training robust domain-agnostic models by leveraging shared information across domains. To learn domain-invariant information, researchers commonly analyze the data generating process (DGP) using structural causal models to design effective algorithms (Arjovsky et al., 2019; Mahajan et al., 2021; Wang & Veitch, 2022). On the contrary, disentanglement is rarely discussed in the sDG literature. This is due to innate conditions of sDG, where only one domain is available for training. This setting makes it hard to apply conventional disentanglement approaches developed in the multi-DG literature. To tackle this, a line of work focuses on how to augment _unseen_ domains effectively with generative models (Volpi et al., 2018; Qiao et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Wan et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2021). However, there is a lack of discussion on whether augmented samples can simulate unseen domains, or whether it can be used to learn domain-invariance. A recent movement in the multi-DG literature highlights the use of pretrained models for OOD generalization, leveraging the knowledge of the pretrained models (Cha et al., 2022; Wortsman et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023). Such works closely resemble the methods introduced in the Knowledge Distillation (KD) literature (Hinton et al., 2015; Adriana et al., 2015; Ahn et al., 2019; Shrivastava et al., 2023; Tian et al., 2020).

## 3 LIMITATIONS OF AUGMENTATION FOR SDG

In this section, we present an overlooked problem of augmentation-based sDG methods. Specifically, we revisit recent works on S-C disentanglement to analyze the effectiveness of utilizing augmentation for out-of-domain generalization.

**A general view towards augmentation-based sDG methods** We present a general expression for augmentation-based sDG methods and discuss their effectiveness. Generally, augmentation-based methods can be expressed as _augment and align_ , minimizing the following objective (omitting some arguments for simplicity) denoting _x_ and _x_ ¯ as an original sample and its augmented view:

Commonly, contrastive loss is used as _L_ MaxEnt. Recently, Von K¨ugelgen et al. (2021) showed that the optimization of a contrastive loss provably minimizes _L_ MaxEnt, learning Φ to extract features that are augment-invariant, under a certain condition. In this perspective, conventional augmentation-based sDG methods could be understood as retrieving augment-invariant features.

**A causal interpretation of data augmentation** We illustrate the underlying data generating process (i.e., DGP) using a causal graph and incorporate data augmentation into the causal graph under the sDG setting. An instance of a given labeled dataset is typically composed of an observation _X_ (i.e., image) and its label _Y_ . Although supervised learning predicts _Y_ directly from _X_ , this does not reflect the underlying causality. We can think of the existence of hidden features (e.g., real-world attributes regarding the subject of the image and the background), which we will refer _W_ , that affect both the image and label. At this moment, the causal graph for DGP can be simply represented as _X ← W → Y_ where _W_ is unobserved.

Now, we incorporate data augmentation into the picture. Given _label-preserving_ augmentation methods, we attain _X_[¯] the augmented view of _X_ . Such an augmentation can be considered as manipulating only the style _S_ (augment-variant) to yield _S_[¯] while retaining its content (augment-invariant) _C_ where _C_ and _S_ partitions _W_ , that is, _W_ = ( _C, S_ ) (see Von K¨ugelgen et al. (2021) for a detailed discussion). Yet, this does not imply that _C_ and _S_ are independent. _C_ causally affects _S_ (also corroborated by experimental results (Klindt et al., 2021)). A way to understand this separation is by viewing such an augmentation as a soft intervention (Eberhardt & Scheines, 2007) on _S_ , resulting in a modified style _S_[¯] . By definition, ( _C, S_[¯] ) becomes the hidden features of _X_[¯] . Furthermore, _C_ consistently affects _Y_ regardless of the _label-preserving_ augmentation. This understanding results in the graph in Figure 1 ( _W_ is implicit) _excluding D_ .

Von K¨ugelgen et al. (2021) showed that, under certain conditions, the above DGP is sound, and augmentation separates _C_ and _S_ . However, the original picture misses an important variable: the domain _D_ . By definition, observations are drawn from the distribution of the domain, thus latent variables _W_ are affected by the domain the data is generated from. Therefore it is unavoidable to incorporate a variable indicating domain _D_ in the figure. In sDG, _D_ is fixed in the sense that we are given just one domain. Due to the single source setting, we cannot distinguish what information is shared across different domains, leaving both _C_ and _S_ potentially affected by _D_ . Hence, unless the discrepancy between the source and target is moderate, optimizing solely the augment-and-align objective (Eq. 1) would be insufficient to address the issue caused by a large domain gap.

**Learning to ignore** To address a large domain shift, we begin with some observations. Conventional _augment and align_ methods are vulnerable to domain shift in the sense that their effectiveness is affected by the augmentation’s proximity to the domain shift. While advanced augmentation methods may simulate small shifts in distribution (e.g., MNIST _→_ USPS in Digits), it is hard to approximate large domain shifts (e.g., PHOTO _→_ SKETCH in PACS) (Section 5.1). If the gap between the source and target domain is large, failure in simulating domain shift would make its augment-invariant features less relevant to domain-invariant features, leading to overfitting to the source domain.

To avoid learning irrelevant features, we can think of a hypothetical regularizer that encourages the model to learn information relevant to domain-invariance, while discouraging domain-specific features. Certainly, this would require a condition that the regularizer be an oracle that can distinguish domain-invariant information. Using this oracle regularizer, we hope to solve the phenomena commonly associated with the large domain gap. Especially, the mid-training fluctuation of OOD performance, which was observed in earlier sDG works (Qiao et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021) but not discussed in-depth.[1] We view that the fluctuation is strongly correlated with the challenge in acquiring domain-invariant features under a large domain gap. We empirically observe that the level of domain gap between the _source_ and _target_ closely matches the magnitude of the mid-train fluctuation, where the increase in domain gap is simultaneously observed with the increase in fluctuation. Detailed information regarding the measure of domain gap is included in Section 5.2. We view mid-training fluctuation as a serious issue since it manifests that the simulated domains do not properly reflect unseen domains and, further, it harms the credibility of learned models due to uncertainty in their real-world performance. In the following section, we search for ways to implement the _hypothetical_ oracle regularizer, inspired by works in knowledge distillation.

## 4 LEVERAGING PRETRAINED MODELS TO LEARN DOMAIN INVARIANCE

We present a novel single source domain generalization method where the aim is to alleviate the issue of mid-train fluctuation. While the general principle of our approach is orthogonal to the type of the data, in this paper, we focus on image data. The overview of our architecture is depicted in Figure 2. At large, the architecture for our method involves three neural networks, a domain generator _G_ , task model classifier _F_ , and an oracle _O_ . We sequentially learn multiple domain generators _{Gk}[K] k_ =1 and use augmented samples created by the generators to train the task model _F_ . More specifically, the generators provide the task model with _challenging_ augmented samples, while the task model guides the generator to create _valid_ augmentations. We train the above process using a combination of two losses: _L_ = _Lf_ + _wg · Lg_ where _Lf_ (Eq. 2) and _Lg_ (Eq. 10) are the loss used to train the task model and the generator, respectively, and _wg ∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ controls the training of _G_ .[2] The exact forms for _Lf_ and _Lg_ will become clear at the end of this section.

We build our method upon the idea that learning domain invariance solely from augmented domains is vulnerable to overfitting to the source, especially when the domain gap is too large to simulate via data augmentation. To alleviate this issue, we propose an oracle regularizer: under the hypothesis that the oracle is capable of generalizing well to unseen domains, we use the oracle to guide the task model to become less domain-dependent. Specifically, our oracle regularization objective regulates the sDG process via an alignment between the hidden feature representation of the task model and the oracle, which we name PROF. In the following section, we elaborate our ideas in depth.

**Notation** We begin by introducing related notation regarding our method. To begin with, calligraphic letters are used to denote state space of a variable. For example, _X_ , _Y_ , and _H_ respectively represent the space of the input image, intermediate feature representation, and labels.
- **Task model:** The task model _F_ = _C ◦ H_ consists of a feature-extractor _H_ : _X →H_ and a classification head _C_ : _H →Y_ .
- **Generator:** A trainable generator _G_ : _X →X_ consists of an encoder-decoder architecture with a style-transfer module placed between the encoder and decoder.

- **Oracle:** The oracle model _O_ = _Co ◦ Ho_ consists of a frozen feature-extractor _Ho_ : _X →H_ and a trainable classification head _Co_ : _H →Y_ . Task model _F_ and oracle model _O_ use separate feature-extractors ( _H_ and _Ho_ ) to map the input data as intermediate representation and pass the representation to the classification head ( _C_ and _Co_ ) for the downstream classification task. For experimental purposes, we match the dimension of representation for the oracle and task model.

- **Distillation Head:** The distillation head _V_ : _H →V_ is used to impose regularization for the task model via oracle’s representation. Instead of directly comparing the intermediate representation in _H_ , representations from _Ho_ and _H_ are mapped through the shared distillation head _V_ , following the analysis of Gupta et al. (2022) on the efficacy of projection heads.
- **Projection Head:** Similar to the distillation head, the task model uses a projection head _P_ : _H →Z_ to project the intermediate representations into a different dimension. The projection

## 4.1 ORACLE REGULARIZER

We devise a novel learning method PROF (Progressive mutual information Regularization for Online distillation of Frozen oracles) to guide the learning process. PROF reformulates the sDG problem under the assumption that if there exists an oracle model _O_ that can generalize well to unseen domains, we can leverage the oracle to learn sDG. The objective for PROF can be formulated as:
where _x_ denotes the original sample and _x_ ¯ the augmented view created by _G_ , _λ_ PROF is a user-set parameter, and Barlow Twins (BT) is defined as (Zbontar et al., 2021):

**Discussion on the Regularization via MI Optimization** The idea of PROF is that we can distill the oracle’s knowledge into the task model by maximizing the shared information between the two models. PROF aims to maximize the MI between the intermediate output features of the two feature-extractors _H_ and _Ho_ . PROF functions as a regularization term that guides the task model from deviating too far from the oracle, encouraging the student task model to learn the oracle’s behavior on data. From this perspective, an intended objective for PROF could be formulated as max _H I_ ( _H_ ( _x_ ); _Ho_ ( _x_ )) where _I_ ( _X_ ; _Y_ ) = E _p_ ( _x,y_ )[log _p_ ( _x | y_ ) _/p_ ( _x_ )] indicates the mutual information (MI). However, directly estimating and optimizing MI are challenging, as the exact estimation of MI is intractable (Paninski, 2003). There exists InfoNCE loss (Oord et al., 2018) which adopts a lower bound of MI (Poole et al., 2019) as a surrogate objective for MI optimization:

However, an issue of InfoNCE as a variational bound of MI is that InfoNCE requires a large batch size for convergence (Shrivastava et al., 2023; Hjelm et al., 2019), making it doubtful for use in small datasets (e.g., PACS). Consequently, we indirectly approximate InfoNCE with a feature decorrelation loss (Zbontar et al., 2021), based on empirical and theoretical results that show its functional proximity (Huang et al., 2021; Tao et al., 2022). Contrary to InfoNCE, the feature decorrelation converges effectively with small batch sizes and large vector dimensions.

Now we discuss the availability of an oracle. In reality, oracles may not be readily available. However, previous studies (Cha et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023) report that models pretrained from a large dataset or with deeper models tend to generalize better at unseen domains. Considering this, we utilize a model pretrained on a larger domain as an oracle. To preserve the knowledge of the oracle, we freeze the feature-extractor _Ho_ of the oracle.

## 4.2 MULTI-DOMAIN ALIGNMENT WITH REDUNDANCY REDUCTION

We now introduce a novel alignment objective MDAR (Multi-Domain Alignment with Redundancy reduction) for sDG. MDAR aims to disentangle latent features that are invariant across multiple augmented views. We design MDAR as a fair baseline of the conventional _augment and align_ method. In learning the _k_ th generator _Gk_ , we create an augmented view _x_ ¯ for a batch of original samples _x_ using the _k_ th generator _Gk_ . We then randomly load two previously learned generators to construct two augmented views _x_ ¯ _[′]_ and _x_ ¯ _[′′]_ . With _{x,_ ¯ _x,_ ¯ _x[′] ,_ ¯ _x[′′] }_ , we encourage their representations vary in a similar way. Hence, we use BT (Eq. 4) over the representations for _{x,_ ¯ _x,_ ¯ _x[′] ,_ ¯ _x[′′] }_ obtained through the projection head and feature extractor, _P ◦ H_ . That is, their cross-correlation matrix _M_ to be closer to an identity matrix. Our alignment loss _L_ MDAR is written as:

where _λ_ MDAR a user-set parameter. Intuitively, via optimizing _L_ MDAR, we can train the task model in a way that multiple views (representations) are aligned. In terms of S-C disentanglement, MDAR encourages the retrieval of augment-invariant features. Different from the commonly used InfoNCE loss, our objective (Eq. 5) does not require negative pairs, thus works well on small batch sizes (Zbontar et al., 2021; Tsai et al., 2021), suitable for benchmarks like PACS.
In our conventional _augment and align_ baseline experiment, we train our model with a variant of Eq. 2: _Lf_ = _Lce_ ( _C_ ( _H_ ( _x_ )) _, y_ ) + _Lcls_ + _w_ MDAR _· L_ MDAR.

## 5 EXPERIMENT

We first present our experimental settings including datasets and architectures. Then, we report experimental results using the accuracy for each target domain, as well as the mean accuracy over all target domains. We designed our experiments to be reproducible.

## 5.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETTINGS

**Datasets** Following the experimental settings in prior sDG works (Qiao et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021), we adopted three broadly used benchmarks for our sDG problem, along with an additional benchmark. **PACS** (Li et al., 2017) is widely used to test the generalizability of trained models against domain shift. It consists of 4 domains of differing styles (Photo, Art, Cartoon, and Sketch) with 7 classes. In default, we train our model with the Photo domain and evaluate the remaining target domains. We also present additional experiments in Appendix A.1. Among the selected benchmarks, PACS is the main target of PROF due to its large gap between domains. **Corrupted CIFAR-10** (i.e. CIFAR-10-C) is a benchmark to test the image classifier robustness under distortion (Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019). We train our model with the train split of the CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky & Hinton, 2009) dataset and test the model accuracy in CIFAR-10-C. We evaluate the robustness of the model with 19 types and 5 levels of corruption. Unlike other benchmarks, we expect that the CIFAR-10-C is sufficient with conventional _augment and align_ methods, as each target domain is created via augmentation of the source domain. **Digits** dataset is a popular benchmark for sDG, comprised of 5 different digit classification datasets, MNIST (Deng, 2012), SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011), MNIST-M (Ganin et al., 2015), SYNDIGIT (Ganin & Lempitsky, 2015), USPS (Le Cun et al., 1989). In our experiment, we train our model with the first 10,000 samples of the MNIST dataset and assess its generalization accuracy across the remaining four domains. **OfficeHome** dataset (Venkateswara et al., 2017) is a common benchmark for DG, but not used for sDG. The benchmark consists of 4 datasets (Real-world, Art, Clipart, Product) with differing styles with 65 classes. We train our model on the Real-world domain and evaluate the remaining domains.

**Implementation** In all experiments, we utilized the identical network architectures used in previous sDG works. For PACS, we adopted AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) pretrained on Imagenet (Russakovsky et al., 2014). For corrupted CIFAR-10, we used a Wide Residual Network (Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2016) of depth 16, and width 4. For Digits, we used the identical network architecture (i.e. conv-pool-conv-pool-fc-fc-softmax) used in previous works. For Office-Home, we used a ResNet18 (He et al., 2016) pretrained on ImageNet-1K dataset (Russakovsky et al., 2014). For an oracle, we selected pretrained models appropriate for each experiment. For PACS and OfficeHome, we chose a RegNetY-16GF (Radosavovic et al., 2020) pretrained on Instagram dataset with SWAG (Supervised Weakly through hashtAGs) (Singh et al., 2022) following experimental reports of Cha et al. (2022); Li et al. (2023). For Corrupted CIFAR-10, we selected an Imagenet pretrained ResNet50. For Digits, we followed the practice of Cha et al. (2022) and used a true oracle pretrained on both the source and target domains of Digits. All oracles are finetuned on the source domain (e.g. Photo, CIFAR-10, MNIST, Real World) and frozen. We test the sensitivity of the hyperparameters using the validation split of the source dataset. Details regarding the training hyperparameters, pretraining process, training process, and the generator module are reported in Appendix B.4, Appendix B.3, Appendix B.2, and Appendix B.1, respectively.

## 5.2 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS AND ANALYSIS

Here we present experimental results over the four benchmark datasets, examination on domain gaps and the effect of PROF.

**Experiment with PACS** The aim of the PACS experiment is to show that PROF functions as a stable regularizer for sDG, reducing the mid-train OOD fluctuation reported in conventional _augment and align_ methods. The results of the PACS experiment are reported in Table 1 where AN, RN, M, and P stands for AlexNet, ResNet18, MDAR, and PROF, respectively.

First, we compare the generalization accuracy. Training AlexNet with PROF (Eq.(2)) showed results close to the current SOTA (Wan et al., 2022) without the use of alignment. Furthermore, we showed state-of-the-art performance in the Sketch domain, where domain gap is considered to be the largest. Similarly, our _augment and align_ baseline using

## 6 CONCLUSION

This paper presents PROF (Progressive mutual information Regularization for Online distillation of Frozen oracles), a novel oracle regularizer to address single source domain generalization under a large domain discrepancy. We underscore the vulnerability of learning robustness via augmentation, which is observed as large fluctuations in the OOD performance during the training process. To mitigate this issue, PROF leverages pretrained oracles to guide the model to learn features that are less domain-specific, via maximization of the feature-level mutual information between the learning model and the oracle. Experiments on multiple datasets (PACS, Digits, Office-Home) demonstrate that PROF can stabilize the fluctuations associated with large domain gaps. We further introduce a strong baseline method with MDAR (Multi-Domain Alignment with Redundancy Reduction) for a fair comparison with PROF. Training with MDAR showed state-of-the-art performance in Digits and displayed a boost in performance when applied to existing methods.

## A EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

## A.1 EXPERIMENTS ON PACS (CONTINUED)

Here we present the results of additional experiments with the PACS benchmark.

Previous experiments on the PACS benchmark only used the Photo dataset as the source domain. In the following section, we report other cases where the source domain is changed (e.g., Art, Cartoon, Sketch). Here, we will denote each experiment as _Art as source_ , _Cartoon as source_ , and _Sketch as source_ , respectively.

In Table 5, we report the sDG accuracy of our two methods, MDAR and PROF, where AN, M, and P stands for AlexNet, MDAR, and PROF, respectively. Each row in the table displays the source domain, backbone type, and the training method (M/P). In cases where Art or Cartoon is used as source domain, training with our oracle regular-

ization PROF marked higher OOD accuracy then its counterpart. On the other hand, PROF suffered when Sketch was set as the source domain, falling behind the baseline MDAR. Our hypothesis is that this behavior is triggered by the subpar performance of the oracle. To elaborate, the oracle used on the _Sketch as source_ experiment displayed low OOD accuracy on the target domains, unsuitable for effective oracle regularization (Photo: 51 _._ 61%, Art: 39 _._ 39%, Cartoon: 56 _._ 85%).

Next, we present the analysis on mid-train OOD fluctuation in each experimental configuration. When the source domain is set as Art, employing PROF resulted in yielded a stabilization of the OOD performance, effectively mitigating fluctuations. The fluctuation was quantified as the reduction in variance across the target domain accuracy in _K >_ 5. When compared with the conventional _augment & align_ method MDAR, our regularization method PROF displayed large reductions in variance (Photo: 1.71 _→_ 1.17, Cartoon: 3.13 _→_ 2.97, Sketch: 21.50 _→_ 11.22). The mid-train OOD fluctuation when source is set as Art, is depicted in Figure 4a.

Similarly, when the source domain is configured as Cartoon, PROF displays similar stabilization of the mid-train OOD performance. Using PROF allows a reduction in fluctuation, measured as variance (Photo: 5.15 _→_ 3.06, Art: 5.00 _→_ 3.07, Sketch: 0.70 _→_ 3.91). We note that the stabilization effect in Sketch is relatively lower than that of other target domains, even lower than our _augment & align_ baseline MDAR. The mid-train fluctuation is demonstrated in Figure 4b.

Lastly, we report the experimental results where the source was set as Sketch. In the _Sketch as source_ experiment, we observe that PROF not only suffers in terms of performance but also exhibits instability. PROF displayed high variance in mid-train performance when compared to the baseline (Photo: 2.46 _→_ 10.41, Art: 2.33 _→_ 7.99, Cartoon: 1.01 _→_ 1.04). The fluctuation is illustrated in Figure 4c. While a clear explanation is absent, we view that this phenomenon is caused by the underperformance of the oracle in the _Sketch as source_ experiment. This result displays a clear example of the problems associated with the obstacles regarding the oracle, where obtaining an oracle may not be readily available. We further discuss the issue with oracles in the following section, Appendix D

This confirms that a gap between the approximated oracle and the true oracle exists, which is a limitation that we acknowledge. We provide further analysis on the oracle in Appendix D

We find that in Digits, increasing the number of simulated domains ( _K_ ) helps OOD generalization. Both our baseline (MDAR) and PROF benefited from long training ( _K >_ 100).

## B IMPLEMENTATION DETAIL

In this section, we report the implementation details of our method.

## B.1 MODEL ARCHITECTURE
We report the details of model architectures used in our experiments. All models were built to match the architecture used in previous studies.

**Task Model** The task model architecture varies in each experiment. For each experiment, we report the feature extractor _H_ , including an additional layer (i.e. buffer) used to match the feature extractor’s output dimension to the oracle’s.

The model used in the PACS experiment is AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012). The model consists of 5 convolutional layers with channels of _{_ 96, 256, 384, 384, 256 _}_ , followed by two fully-connected layers of size 4096 units. The buffer is a 2-layered MLP that maps the output dimension 4096 to that of the oracle (RegNetY-16GF), which is 3024. Hence, the final output dimension of the feature extractor is 3024.

The model used in the Corrupted CIFAR-10 experiment is a Wide Residual Network (i.e. WRN) of width _w_ = 4 and depth 16 (Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2016). WRN is a model that boosts its performance by widening the network by a certain factor _w_ . The model consists of 4 network blocks with channels incrementally increasing as _{_ 16 _,_ 16 _w,_ 32 _w,_ 64 _w}_ . Specifically, the 4 blocks refer to an initial convolutional layer, followed by three additional network blocks. We further follow the original WRN design and set the dropout rate as 0 _._ 3. The buffer is a 2-layered MLP that maps the output dimension 256 to that of the oracle (ResNet50), which is 2048. Hence, the final output dimension of the feature extractor is 2048.

For the model used in the Digits experiment, please refer to Section 5.1. The architecture consists of two 5 × 5 convolutional layers, with 64 and 128 channels respectively. Each convolutional layer is followed by a MaxPooling layer (2 × 2). The network also includes two fully connected layers with sizes of 1024, 1024 being the final output dimension of the feature extractor. Since we do not employ oracle for the Digits experiment, a buffer was not added.

**Generator** In this section, we describe the generator in detail. While the design of the generator slightly varies in each experiment, the basic architecture is the same. The generator consists of an encoder and a decoder, with a spatial transformer network (STN) and a style-transfer module in between the encoder and the decoder. The four components are placed in the order of Encoder - STN - Style-Transfer - Decoder.

We begin by illustrating the overall process of how an image is augmented by the generator. First, the input image is passed through the encoder to get a feature representation vector. The feature vector is then passed through the STN and the style-transfer module for modification. The modified vector is then reconstructed via a decoder, returning an augmented image. The mentioned process is illustrated in Figure 8. In the figure, we depict how each module modifies the input image.

STN is a module that learns to perform spatial transformations on the input (Jaderberg et al., 2015). During the process, the STN module learns transformation parameters, where the parameters each define the magnitude of spatial transformations (e.g., rotation, scaling, translation). The STN module can be inserted at any point in the generator, allowing the generator to selectively transform the data up to a degree that is label-preserving. We place the STN right after the Encoder, following the experimental results of the original paper (Jaderberg et al., 2015). In Figure 8, we can see that the STN performs spatial transformations, creating the modified image at the middle. An advantage of STN is that no additional requirements are needed for training the module.

The style-transfer module modifies the features of the input image by adjusting the mean and standard deviation of the image features. This is performed using a normalization technique called Batch-Instance Normalization (i.e. BIN) (Nam & Kim, 2018). BIN selectively normalizes the features of the input image that are of less significance, while preserving features that are important. Note that this module is a modified version of the AdaIN method introduced in Huang & Belongie (2017), where we switched the normalization method from Instance Normalization (Ulyanov et al., 2016) to BIN for effective style transfer.

We share the results of applying these modifications in Figure 9. Whilst previous augmentation methods (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021) were limited to manipulating certain attributes (e.g., color, stroke), our method further allows spatial manipulations (e.g., shape, location). For instance, in the right image of Figure 9, we can observe that the images generated using our method displayed a large variance in shape, position, and color. This modification is inspired by recent studies on domain shift (Kaur et al., 2022; Wiles et al., 2021), which revealed that domain shift occurs on a variety of levels. However, an observable limitation is that the STN cannot transform complex images as in PACS, as small spatial modifications vastly change the semantics of the image. As depicted in Figure 10, the effect of the spatial modification is limited on PACS images.

**Oracle** Here, we report the architecture of the oracle. The oracle varies on the type of the experiment, (1) a RegNetY-16GF for the PACS and Office-Home experiment, (2) a ResNet50 for the corrupted CIFAR-10 experiment.

The RegNetY-16GF is a variant of the RegNet family, a line of models introduced in (Radosavovic et al., 2020) for image classification. The name of the model indicates its configurations, where the ”Y” indicates the convolution method, and the ”16GF” represents the model’s capacity or complexity. We implement the model, and its model weights using the torchvision (Falbel, 2023) library. We used the weights pretrained via end-to-end fine-tuning of the original SWAG (Singh et al., 2022) weights on the ImageNet-1K data (Russakovsky et al., 2014). We then fine-tuned the pretrained model again with the Photo domain of PACS for 200 epochs, with a learning rate of 1 _e −_ 4 using

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a critical gap in Single-source Domain Generalization (sDG) by questioning the implicit assumption that augmentation-invariance is equivalent to domain-invariance. The authors provide a strong conceptual framework by connecting sDG to Style-Content (S-C) disentanglement and causal inference, which offers a more rigorous foundation than many existing heuristic-based augmentation papers. The introduction of PROF and MDAR appears to be a timely integration of pretrained "oracles" into the sDG pipeline, moving beyond simple data synthesis toward knowledge distillation. However, the clarity of the paper is somewhat hindered by the ambitious scope of its contributions; introducing a theoretical analysis, a new regularization method (PROF), and a new baseline objective (MDAR) all at once risks a fragmented narrative. Specifically, the link between the S-C disentanglement theory (Section 3) and the specific mechanics of mutual information regularization in PROF needs to be explicitly bridged to avoid the method feeling like a disconnected heuristic. Additionally, while the paper identifies "instability" and "fluctuations" as key issues in sDG, the most significant challenge will be demonstrating that PROF provides a statistically significant improvement over strong, modern baselines that already utilize pretrained weights (e.g., CLIP-based models), as the "oracle" advantage might stem more from the pretraining data than the proposed regularization logic.

comments.title: Causal Graph Inconsistency
comments.quote: Therefore it is unavoidable to incorporate a variable indicating domain D in the figure. In sDG, D is fixed in the sense that we are given just one domain. Due to the single source setting, we cannot distinguish what information is shared across different domains, leaving both C and S potentially affected by D.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors argue that the Domain D affects the latent variables W = (C, S). However, the causal diagram in Figure 1 appears to show D as an ancestor of both C and S. This configuration implies that the 'content' C is itself domain-dependent. If D -> C -> Y, then the 'content' learned in one domain might not exist or might change in another, which undermines the theoretical goal of retrieving 'domain-invariant' information. The authors should clarify if D affects only S (the common assumption) or if they are proposing a model where even the content is confounded by the domain.

comments.title: Missing and Conflicting Definitions of L_cls and Oracle Integration
comments.quote: Lcls is a cross-entropy loss that assures the validity of the generated samples x̄ based on predictions from task model F (also from oracle O if PROF is employed.)
comments.explanation: There is a discrepancy in how L_cls is defined and applied. In Section 4, it is included in the task model loss L_f, but Equation 6 (the classification loss for augmented views) is missing from the snippets. Furthermore, the prose states that if PROF is employed, L_cls should ensure consistency with the Oracle (O). However, the formal logic provided only references the task model F and ground truth y. This leaves the mathematical role of the Oracle in the label-preservation objective underspecified and critical for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent Logic and Scope of Adversarial Loss (L_adv)
comments.quote: Ladv is an adversarial loss function designed to reverse the alignment process by negating the feature-decorrelation loss used in Eq. 4.
comments.explanation: The paper states L_adv 'negates' the Barlow Twins (BT) loss to encourage diversity. However, the BT loss consists of an invariance term and a redundancy reduction term (off-diagonal). Negating the entire sum incentivizes the generator to increase cross-feature redundancy, which is a questionable objective for domain shift simulation. Additionally, Eq. 9 defines L_adv for a single pair (x, x̄), whereas the MDAR loss it is meant to 'negate' aligns four views. It is unclear if L_adv targets all views or just the current pair.

comments.title: Terminological Ambiguity and Contradiction Regarding 'Oracle'
comments.quote: Since we do not employ oracle for the Digits experiment, a buffer was not added.
comments.explanation: There is a direct contradiction regarding the use of the Oracle. For Digits, Section 4 claims the use of a true oracle (pretrained on source and target), while Section 7 explicitly states an oracle was not employed. Furthermore, for other datasets, the 'Oracle' is actually a RegNetY pretrained on Instagram (a 'Pretrained Proxy'). This inconsistency makes the 'Ours P' results in Table 3 un-reproducible and risks overstating the claim of oracle guidance.

comments.title: Numerical and Structural Inconsistency in Feature Dimensions
comments.quote: The buffer is a 2-layered MLP that maps the output dimension 256 to that of the oracle (ResNet50), which is 2048. Hence, the final output dimension of the feature extractor is 2048.
comments.explanation: In the Corrupted CIFAR-10 experiment, the authors state the feature extractor output is 2048. However, the backbone (WRN-16-4) typically outputs 256. This implies the 'buffer' is being treated as part of the feature extractor despite the PROF loss being defined as acting on a separate distillation head V. It is unclear if V and the buffer are the same entity or how the distillation dimensions are managed across different architectures.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Hyperparameter Notation and Scaling
comments.quote: We optimize the hyperparameters wadv , wcyc , wdiv using grid search... switching between λ = 0.005 and p [1] posed no notable impact.
comments.explanation: There is a notation shift between the formal equations (using λ for generator weights) and the sensitivity analysis (using w prefixes for the generator and λ for the task model). Furthermore, the text suggests λ is interchangeable with 'p [1]' (likely 1/p, where p is the hidden dimension). Since p ranges from 128 to 2048, 1/p is a vastly different scale than 0.005. The lack of clear scaling or consistent naming makes the sensitivity analysis difficult to verify.

comments.title: Mathematical Ambiguity in Entropy and Diversity Losses
comments.explanation: The authors state that contrastive loss 'minimizes' L_MaxEnt (an entropy-regularized objective), which is sign-ambiguous as contrastive proxies usually maximize Mutual Information bounds. Similarly, $L_{div}$ is defined as a simple negated L2-norm. Without a margin or clipping mechanism, minimizing a negative norm is mathematically ill-posed for an optimizer as the generator can minimize the loss by driving the distance to infinity (producing noise).

comments.title: Causal Graph Inconsistency
comments.quote: Therefore it is unavoidable to incorporate a variable indicating domain D in the figure. In sDG, D is fixed in the sense that we are given just one domain. Due to the single source setting, we cannot distinguish what information is shared across different domains, leaving both C and S potentially affected by D.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors argue that the Domain $D$ affects the latent variables $W = (C, S)$. However, looking at the provided 'picture text' for Figure 1, the graph structure is described as including $D, S, C, \bar{S}, X, Y$, and $\bar{X}$. In standard Causal Domain Generalization literature, if $D$ is a parent of $S$ (style) but the goal is to find features $C$ that are invariant, $C$ should typically be d-separated from $D$ or be the causal parent of $Y$. If $D \to C$, then by definition $C$ is domain-variant, which contradicts the authors' goal of 'retrieving domain-invariant information'. While the authors acknowledge that in sDG we cannot *distinguish* what is shared, the causal diagram in Figure 1 (as reconstructed from the OCR text) appears to show $D$ as an ancestor of both $C$ and $S$. This configuration implies that the 'content' $C$ is itself domain-dependent in the source domain. If $D \to C \to Y$, then the 'content' learned in one domain might not exist or might change in another, which undermines the theoretical basis of using augmentation to find 'invariant' features. The authors should clarify if $D$ affects only $S$ (the common assumption where style is domain-specific) or if they are proposing a model where even the content is confounded by the domain.

comments.title: Missing definition of $L_{cls}$ in Task Model Loss
comments.quote: $L_f = L_{ce} ( C ( H ( x ) ) , y ) + L_{cls} + w_{MDAR} \cdot L_{MDAR}$
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors define the task model loss $L_f$. They mention $L_{ce}$ as the cross-entropy loss for the original sample $x$. They then include $L_{cls}$ as a separate term. While the text in Section 3 mentions '$L_{cls}$ ; Equation (6)', Equation 6 is not provided in the snippet, and the prose in Section 3 identifies $L_{ce}$ as the cross-entropy loss formula. Usually, in these architectures, $L_{cls}$ refers to the cross-entropy loss on the *augmented* view $\bar{x}$. However, because the authors are using multiple generators $G_1...G_k$, it is unclear if $L_{cls}$ is an average over all generated views, only the current view $\bar{x}$, or includes the views $\bar{x}'$ and $\bar{x}''$ used in MDAR. Given that the paper emphasizes 'stabilizing' the process, the exact formulation of how the classification loss is applied to augmented views is critical for reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential contradiction in $L_{MaxEnt}$ and Contrastive Loss relationship
comments.quote: Commonly, contrastive loss is used as $L_{MaxEnt}$. Recently, Von K¨ugelgen et al. (2021) showed that the optimization of a contrastive loss provably minimizes $L_{MaxEnt}$, learning Φ to extract features that are augment-invariant
comments.explanation: The authors state that contrastive loss *minimizes* $L_{MaxEnt}$. However, they define $L_{MaxEnt}$ as an objective that aligns representations *under entropy regularization*. In information theoretic approaches to contrastive learning (like InfoNCE), the objective is usually seen as *maximizing* a lower bound on Mutual Information (which involves an entropy term), or minimizing a contrastive proxy. If $L_{MaxEnt}$ is an entropy-regularized alignment, 'minimizing' it might imply reducing entropy or reducing alignment. Usually, the goal is to maximize the entropy of the representations (to avoid collapse) while minimizing the distance between matched pairs. The phrasing 'minimizes $L_{MaxEnt}$' is slightly ambiguous—it is unclear if $L_{MaxEnt}$ is a loss function (to be minimized) or a measure of entropy/alignment (where the relationship to the proxy loss needs to be sign-consistent).

comments.title: Mismatched label-preservation loss term
comments.quote: Lcls is a cross-entropy loss that assures the validity of the generated samples x̄ based on predictions from task model F (also from oracle O if PROF is employed.)
comments.explanation: In Equation (6), the second term of the loss is defined as I(F(x) = y) · Lce(F(x̄), y). The text explains that if PROF is employed, Lcls should also ensure consistency with the oracle O. However, the formal definition of Lcls in Eq. (6) only references predictions from the task model F and the ground truth label y. There is no mathematical term in the provided equations for Lcls or Lg that incorporates O's prediction into the label-preserving objective for the generator, which contradicts the prose. If the oracle is meant to 'guide the generator to create valid augmentations' as claimed in Section 4, the definition of Lcls is underspecified or missing the oracle component.

comments.title: Missing index on the adversarial loss term
comments.quote: Ladv is an adversarial loss function designed to reverse the alignment process by negating the feature-decorrelation loss used in Eq. 4.
comments.explanation: Equation (9) defines Ladv using the indicator function I(F(x) = y), effectively only applying the adversarial shift to samples that are correctly classified. However, the actual loss term used is BT(P(H(x)), P(H(x̄))). While the text says this is used to 'negate the feature-decorrelation loss used in Eq. 4', Eq. 4 (the general BT loss) and Eq. 5 (the MDAR loss) use different feature mappings. MDAR uses the projection head P, whereas PROF (which relies on Eq. 4) uses the distillation head V. Since the generator loss Lg in Eq. 10 includes Ladv specifically 'if MDAR is used', the reader would expect Ladv to target the same heads as L_MDAR. However, the definition of Ladv in Eq. (9) only includes one pair (x, x̄), whereas MDAR (Eq. 5) aligns four views. It is unclear if Ladv is intended to negate the alignment for all views in the MDAR memory bank or just the current pair, which affects the reproducibility of the 'adversarial' generator training.

comments.title: Unclear logic for 'negating' feature-decorrelation
comments.quote: Ladv is an adversarial loss function designed to reverse the alignment process by negating the feature-decorrelation loss used in Eq. 4.
comments.explanation: The paper states that Ladv 'negates' the BT loss to encourage diversity/domain shift. In Equation (9), Ladv is defined as -BT(...). While mathematically this is a negation, the BT loss (Eq. 4) consists of two parts: an invariance term (diagonal) and a redundancy reduction term (off-diagonal). Simply negating the entire sum means the generator is incentivized to *increase* redundancy (the off-diagonal correlations) as much as it is incentivized to *decrease* feature invariance (the diagonal). Usually, in adversarial domain augmentation (like Volpi et al. 2018 or Qiao et al. 2020), the goal is to find 'hard' examples by maximizing the task loss or minimizing similarity, but maximizing redundancy (the second term of BT) is a strange objective for a generator. The loss might be better defined as only negating the alignment part of BT, or the authors should clarify why maximizing cross-feature redundancy is desirable for a domain shift simulator.

comments.title: Terminological ambiguity in 'Oracle' definition vs. implementation
comments.quote: Specifically, our oracle regularization objective regulates the sDG process via an alignment between the hidden feature representation of the task model and the oracle, which we name PROF.
comments.explanation: The authors define an 'Oracle' as a model capable of generalizing well to unseen domains. However, in the 'Experiment' section, they reveal that for PACS and OfficeHome, the 'oracle' is actually a RegNetY-16GF pretrained on the Instagram dataset with SWAG. While they justify this by citing studies that larger/deeper models generalize better, the term 'Oracle' usually implies access to ground truth or target domain information (which they do use for the Digits dataset). For the other datasets, it is technically a 'Pretrained Proxy'. While the authors are transparent about this choice, there is a risk of overstating the claim that the model is guided by an 'Oracle' when it is actually being guided by a different architecture's inductive bias from a larger source dataset. This could mislead a reader into thinking target-domain knowledge was leaked during training, whereas it is actually just transfer learning from a massive-scale proxy.

comments.title: Potential sign error or ambiguity in Diversity Loss
comments.explanation: In Section 4.3, the authors state they 'encourage the generator to create diverse augmentations' and define $L_{div}$ as the 'negated L2-norm between two augmented views'. Generally, in optimization, one minimizes a loss. Minimizing a negative norm ($-||a-b||$) is equivalent to maximizing the distance between the views, which correctly encourages diversity. However, the summary lists $L_g = L_{cls} + \lambda_{cyc}L_{cyc} + \lambda_{div}L_{div} + \lambda_{adv}L_{adv}$. If $\lambda_{div}$ is positive, this works. Often, diversity losses in literature (like the one cited from Li et al. 2021) use a ratio or a bounded distance to prevent instability. Simply minimizing a negative norm can be divergent (the generator can make the distance infinite by producing noise). The text doesn't specify if there is a margin or a clipping mechanism to ensure the 'diversity' doesn't destroy the 'validity' ($L_{cls}$ and $L_{cyc}$), though the presence of the weighted sum implies a trade-off. It is a minor point, but without a bound, $L_{div}$ is mathematically ill-posed for a standard optimizer.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Hyperparameter Notation
comments.quote: We explore our method’s sensitivity to hyperparameters. ( _λ_ PROF): _λ_ PROF is the hyperparameter used for PROF that operates as the balancing weight of the two functions in Equation (4). ... ( _λ_ MDAR and _λadv_ ): The study on _λ_ MDAR and _λadv_ is processed similar to _λ_ PROF. ... ( _wadv_ , _wcyc_ , _wdiv_ ): We optimize the hyperparameters _wadv_ , _wcyc_ , _wdiv_ using grid search.
comments.explanation: There is a discrepancy between the notation used in the formal definitions/equations and the hyperparameter study in Section A.5. In the 'Key Equations' summary and Section 4.3, the weights for the generator loss ($L_g$) are defined using $\lambda$ (e.g., $\lambda_{cyc}$, $\lambda_{div}$, $\lambda_{adv}$). Furthermore, the task model total loss uses $w$ as a prefix (e.g., $w_{PROF}$, $w_{MDAR}$). However, in Section A.5, the authors refer to $\lambda_{PROF}$ and $\lambda_{MDAR}$ as the balancing weights for the alignment functions, while shifting to $w$ prefixes ($w_{adv}$, $w_{cyc}$, $w_{div}$) for the generator. This is confusing because $\lambda_{PROF}$ was previously defined in the PROF objective as an internal weight for the augmented view term, while $w_{PROF}$ was the overall objective weight. A reader attempting to reproduce the sensitivity analysis would be unsure if the authors are varying the trade-off between original/augmented views or the importance of the module relative to the classification loss.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Reciprocal Hyperparameters
comments.quote: Switching between _λ_ = 0 _._ 005 and _p_[1][posed no notable impact on the] learning process, where _p_ is the length of a vector in _P_ (projection head output space).
comments.explanation: The authors suggest an alternative hyperparameter value for $\lambda$ based on the dimensionality of the projection/distillation head. The text says 'p [1]' (likely an OCR error for $1/p$). In SSL literature, specifically Barlow Twins (Zbontar et al. 2021), the redundancy reduction term is often scaled by $1/d$. If the authors mean $\lambda = 1/p$, the text is currently too garbled to be certain. Given that $p$ (the dimension of $P$) is typically 128 to 2048, $1/p$ is a significantly different scale than 0.005. Asserting the method is 'resilient' to this switch without providing the actual values or a clear formula for the second candidate makes the claim difficult to verify or reproduce.

comments.title: Subscript Inconsistency for Digits Target Domains
comments.quote: In SVHN and SYNDIGIT (S-D), we show large improvement, while results in MNIST-M (M-M) show slight deficiency.
comments.explanation: In Section 5.2 and the passage text, the authors use 'S-D' and 'M-M' as abbreviations for SYNDIGIT and MNIST-M. However, in Table 3 and the surrounding analysis of mid-train fluctuation, they frequently switch between 'S-D' and 'SYN', and 'M-M' and 'MM'. For example: 'MM-M MM-P' vs 'SYN-M SYN-P' in Figure 5 text. While a reader can infer these refer to (Domain)-(Method), the toggling between 'S-D' and 'SYN' could lead to confusion with 'S' (Style features) defined in the notation section, especially since 'S' also stands for Sketch in the PACS experiments. Standardizing the domain abbreviations is necessary for clarity in a paper with multiple benchmarks using similar initials.

comments.title: Contradiction regarding Oracle use in Digits experiment
comments.quote: Since we do not employ oracle for the Digits experiment, a buffer was not added.
comments.explanation: There is a direct contradiction between the architectural description in Section 7 and the experimental results/methodology described in Section 4. In Section 4 (Experiment with Digits), the authors explicitly state: 'we follow the practice of Cha et al. (2022) and use a true oracle, a model pretrained on both the source and target domains. Our method with PROF showed a large drop...'. PROF by definition requires an oracle to calculate $L_{PROF}$. However, the text in Section 7 explicitly states an oracle was not employed and no buffer was added for Digits. If PROF was indeed run (as shown in Table 3 and the variance reduction stats), an oracle and a dimension-matching buffer would have been necessary. This makes the implementation details of the 'Ours P' result in Table 3 un-reproducible and logically inconsistent with the text.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in feature dimensions
comments.quote: The buffer is a 2-layered MLP that maps the output dimension 256 to that of the oracle (ResNet50), which is 2048. Hence, the final output dimension of the feature extractor is 2048.
comments.explanation: In the Corrupted CIFAR-10 experiment, the authors use a Wide Residual Network (WRN-16-4). According to the text and standard architecture (Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2016), the final block has $64w$ channels. For $w=4$, this equals 256. However, WRN-16-4 typically performs global average pooling at the end of the final block, resulting in a feature vector of size 256. The text states the buffer maps 256 to 2048 and then says 'the final output dimension of the feature extractor is 2048'. This is ambiguous: usually, the 'feature extractor' $H$ refers to the backbone. If the 'final output' is 2048, it implies the buffer is considered part of the feature extractor for the distillation task. More importantly, ResNet50's penultimate layer (after global average pooling) is 2048, but the summary defines the PROF loss as acting on the output of a distillation head $V$, which maps $H$ and $H_o$ to a shared space of dimension $D$. The text here implies the distillation happens directly at 2048, which might conflict with the 'distillation head $V$' mentioned in the definitions. It's unclear if $V$ is the buffer or if they are separate entities.

comments.title: Potential OCR error or naming inconsistency for Optimizer
comments.quote: trained for 100 fine-tuning epochs, with a learning rate of 1 e − 4 with the SDG optimizer
comments.explanation: The text lists 'SDG optimizer' twice (once for ResNet50 and once for RegNetY). Standard literature and the 'References' section (Kingma & Ba) refer to Stochastic Gradient Descent as 'SGD'. Given the context that the authors refer to 'SGD' in the PACS/RegNet description just a few lines prior ('1 e − 4 using the SGD optimizer'), the use of 'SDG' is likely an OCR misread or a typo for 'SGD'. However, if 'SDG' refers to a specific variant not defined, it would be underspecified. Given 'SGD' is used in the same paragraph for the same purpose, I assume this is a typo/OCR error for SGD, but it appears consistently enough to warrant a check for consistency.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and conceptually well-motivated investigation into the limitations of augmentation-based methods in single-source domain generalization (sDG), offering both a critical analysis and a novel solution in the form of the PROF regularization framework. The writing is generally clear, with a strong theoretical grounding in causal modeling, style-content disentanglement, and domain generalization, and the paper effectively leverages recent insights from contrastive learning and mutual information minimization to challenge assumptions underlying existing sDG approaches. The central claim—that augmentation invariance does not necessarily imply domain invariance—is both significant and convincingly argued through a causal lens, contributing valuable conceptual clarity to the field. However, a major concern lies in the partial presentation of key components: the objectives and mechanisms introduced—particularly the MDAR baseline and the full PROF formulation—are cut off mid-definition in the provided manuscript, undermining the ability to fully assess the technical soundness and novelty of the proposed methods. Additionally, while the motivation is strong, the empirical validation appears insufficiently detailed in the excerpt, with no results presented to substantiate the claim of "large fluctuations in OOD performance" or the stabilization effect of PROF. The reliance on pretrained models as "oracles" is promising but requires deeper discussion on scalability, domain mismatch between pretraining and downstream tasks, and computational cost. Overall, the paper shows strong potential due to its insightful critique and innovative direction, but its impact is currently limited by incomplete technical exposition and a lack of empirical grounding in the submitted portion.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Augment-and-Align Objective
comments.quote: Generally, augmentation-based methods can be expressed as _augment and align_ , minimizing the following objective (omitting some arguments for simplicity) denoting _x_ and _x_ ¯ as an original sample and its augmented view:
comments.explanation: The passage implies that a formal objective function will follow, but no explicit equation is provided despite references to components like _Lce_ and _L_MaxEnt_ later in the text. This creates a structural gap in exposition, undermining clarity at a foundational point in the paper. While the objective may appear elsewhere (e.g., as Eq. 1), its absence here—especially when naming constituent terms—leads to underspecification and risks misleading readers about the completeness of the formulation.

comments.title: Inconsistency Between Mutual Information Goal and Barlow Twins Use
comments.quote: PROF aims to maximize the MI between the intermediate output features of the two feature-extractors _H_ and _Ho_ . PROF functions as a regularization term that guides the task model from deviating too far from the oracle... max _H I_ ( _H_ ( _x_ ); _Ho_ ( _x_ ))... However, directly estimating and optimizing MI are challenging... Consequently, we indirectly approximate InfoNCE with a feature decorrelation loss (Zbontar et al., 2021)...
comments.explanation: The authors claim that Barlow Twins (BT) is used to maximize mutual information (MI), but this is an overstatement. While BT reduces redundancy by decorrelating features, it does not optimize a tight lower bound on MI like InfoNCE. It enforces a specific structure on cross-correlation, which is empirically aligned with MI objectives but fundamentally different. The paper overreaches by equating BT to MI maximization without clarifying the theoretical divergence or the inductive biases involved, despite citing supportive work. This substitution lacks sufficient justification for conceptual equivalence.

comments.title: Underspecified Oracle Regularization Mechanism
comments.quote: The distillation head _V_ : _H →V_ is used to impose regularization for the task model via oracle’s representation. Instead of directly comparing the intermediate representation in _H_ , representations from _Ho_ and _H_ are mapped through the shared distillation head _V_ , following the analysis of Gupta et al. (2022) on the efficacy of projection heads.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces a shared distillation head _V_ to align representations from _H_ and _Ho_, but fails to specify key technical details: whether _V_ is applied to both models, how it is trained (trainable or frozen), and how the loss _L_PROF_ is computed over the projected features. The distinction between a frozen oracle and a trainable adapter head is critical, and without clarity on whether gradients flow through _V_, the method is underspecified. Reproducibility is compromised because the design and optimization of _V_ significantly affect distillation performance.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation Between PROF and MDAR Loss Formulations
comments.quote: We train the task model _F_ using a weighted combination of multiple losses, namely, the crossentropy classification loss of _x_ ( _Lce_ ) and _x_ ¯ ( _Lcls_ ; Equation (6)), with _L_ PROF and _L_ MDAR written as: where _w_ PROF and _w_ MDAR is a user-set parameter...
comments.explanation: The passage introduces both _Lce_ and _Lcls_ as classification losses but uses different symbols without explanation. Given that _Lce_ is formally defined earlier and _Lcls_ is not, and both apply to augmented data, the inconsistent notation risks misleading readers into thinking they are different loss types. While likely identical in form, the lack of clarification violates standard notational consistency. This issue is compounded by missing equations (e.g., Equation 6), further obscuring the loss structure.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Generator Training Control Parameter wg
comments.quote: _wg ∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ controls the training of _G_ .[2] The exact forms for _Lf_ and _Lg_ will become clear at the end of this section.
comments.explanation: The parameter _wg_ is described as a binary switch for generator training, but its scheduling across multiple generators _Gk_ and training epochs is underspecified. It's unclear whether _wg_ is per-generator or global, how generators are sequenced, and whether the transition from 1 to 0 is abrupt or annealed. This affects reproducibility and raises questions about the potential impact of halting generator updates mid-training. The lack of synchronization details with the task model training weakens the method description.

comments.title: Incorrect cross-entropy loss formula due to OCR corruption
comments.explanation: The formula appears corrupted, with broken LaTeX structure (e.g., '[] _i[y][i]_'). The intended expression is almost certainly the standard cross-entropy loss $\mathcal{L}_{ce}(\mathbf{y}, \hat{\mathbf{y}}) = -\sum_i y[i] \log(\hat{y}[i])$, but the OCR corruption renders it mathematically invalid. While recoverable by experts, it misleads less experienced readers and hinders correct interpretation.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of L_MaxEnt and its relation to contrastive loss
comments.quote: and _L_ MaxEnt is an objective that simultaneously aligns the mapped representations Φ( _x_ ) and Φ(¯ _x_ ) under entropy regularization, where Φ is a feature extractor. Commonly, contrastive loss is used as _L_ MaxEnt.
comments.explanation: The term '_L_MaxEnt_' implies a maximum entropy objective, but contrastive loss typically aims to align positive pairs, which reduces entropy in representation space. The conflation of 'maximum entropy' with contrastive learning is misleading without clarification. The authors suggest InfoNCE-style losses are used, but equating them directly to _L_MaxEnt_ without theoretical justification risks misrepresenting the underlying mechanism. Since this is a foundational definition, the lack of precision could mislead readers.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the Set of Representations for MDAR Loss
comments.quote: With \{x,\bar , x,\bar , x[\prime] ,\bar x[\prime\prime] \} , we encourage their representations vary in a similar way. Hence, we use BT (Eq. 4) over the representations for \{x,\bar , x,\bar , x[\prime] ,\bar x[\prime\prime] \} obtained through the projection head and feature extractor, P ◦ H.
comments.explanation: The notation \{\bar{x}, \bar{x}, \bar{x}', \bar{x}''\} duplicates \bar{x} twice, suggesting either a typo or unintended repetition. In standard Barlow Twins usage, distinct augmented views are used for positive pairs. Duplicating \bar{x} would artificially inflate the cross-correlation matrix diagonal and distort regularization. A more plausible intention is \{x, \bar{x}, \bar{x}', \bar{x}''\}, but as written, the set is ambiguous and risks incorrect implementation.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation for Feature Extractors in Oracle and Task Model
comments.quote: Task model F and oracle model O use separate feature-extractors ( H and Ho ) to map the input data as intermediate representation and pass the representation to the classification head ( C and Co ) for the downstream classification task.
comments.explanation: The notation overloads _H_ as both the feature extractor function and its codomain space, which is common but becomes problematic when combined with _Ho_ not being explicitly mapped to the same space. More critically, in the PROF objective, _H_ is used as an optimization variable: _max_H I(H(x); Ho(x))_, mixing function, space, and optimization variable in one symbol. This hierarchy confusion is amplified by OCR-related ambiguity between calligraphic and regular fonts, risking misunderstanding for less experienced readers.

comments.title: Unclear Application Scope of Barlow Twins in MDAR Loss
comments.quote: we use BT (Eq. 4) over the representations for \{x,\bar , x,\bar , x[\prime] ,\bar x[\prime\prime] \} obtained through the projection head and feature extractor, P ◦ H . That is, their cross-correlation matrix M to be closer to an identity matrix. Our alignment loss L_MDAR is written as:
comments.explanation: Barlow Twins is traditionally applied to pairs of augmented views, but here it is applied to a set of four representations. It's unclear how the cross-correlation matrix _M_ is computed—for example, whether it is averaged over all pairs or computed over a concatenated batch. The text implies a single matrix but does not describe aggregation. This underspecification affects reproducibility, as different implementations could lead to divergent behaviors.

comments.title: Potential Misuse of Mutual Information Definition
comments.quote: I(X;Y) = E_{p(x,y)}[log p(x|y)/p(x)]
comments.explanation: The definition appears nonstandard: while algebraically equivalent to the standard form under expectation (due to symmetry), it is written as _log p(x|y)/p(x)_, which simplifies to _log p(y|x)/p(y)_, making it effectively _I(Y;X)_. Although correct numerically, the formulation is unconventional and could confuse readers unfamiliar with mutual information identities. It risks implying a misunderstanding of the canonical form, despite correct citations.

comments.title: Missing Definition of Representations in PROF Objective
comments.quote: The objective for PROF can be formulated as: where x denotes the original sample and x̄ the augmented view created by G, λ_PROF is a user-set parameter, and Barlow Twins (BT) is defined as (Zbontar et al., 2021):
comments.explanation: The text claims the PROF objective 'can be formulated as' but provides no equation. The intended formula is absent, likely due to OCR truncation. It’s unclear whether BT is applied to (H(x), Ho(x)), (H(x), Ho(x̄)), or another combination. While context suggests alignment of _H(x)_ and _Ho(x)_, the absence of an equation prevents verification. This renders the method underspecified and unreproducible from the given passage.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed average in CIFAR-10-C table and computed value
comments.explanation: The reported average for Ours M is 74.49, but the actual average of (77.10, 76.35, 67.94, 76.57) is (77.10 + 76.35 + 67.94 + 76.57)/4 = 309.96/4 = 77.49. The value 74.49 is likely a typo. This is a numerical inconsistency in a key result table, undermining credibility in baseline comparisons.

comments.title: Incorrect average claimed in Digits experiment table
comments.explanation: In Table 3, Ours P reports an average of 81.82, but the computed average of (76.24, 81.82, 78.67, 88.79) is 325.52/4 = 81.38. The reported average exactly matches the M-M accuracy (81.82), suggesting the 'Avg.' cell was accidentally filled with the domain-specific result. This is a formatting or clerical error that misrepresents performance and contradicts other correctly computed averages in the same table.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of $\lambda_{\text{PROF}}$ and $\lambda$ in Barlow Twins
comments.quote:
where $\lambda_{	ext{PROF}}$ is a user-set parameter, and Barlow Twins (BT) is defined as (Zbontar et al., 2021):

$$L_{	ext{BT}}(z, z^+) = \sum_i (1 - M_{ii})^2 + \lambda \sum_i \sum_{j
eq i} M_{ij}^2$$
comments.explanation: The hyperparameter $\lambda$ within the BT loss is distinct from $\lambda_{\text{PROF}}$, which scales the entire PROF loss. However, the notation invites confusion by using $\lambda$ without disambiguating it from $\lambda_{\text{PROF}}$. Given that $\lambda_{\text{PROF}}$ has just been introduced, a reader might mistakenly believe it controls the off-diagonal term in BT. The original BT formulation uses a fixed $\beta$ (often 1), so using a variable $\lambda$ as a tunable hyperparameter—without naming or justification—further compounds the issue.

comments.title: Unclear Generator Training Dynamics and Their Relation to $K$
comments.quote: We sequentially train multiple generators $\{G_k\}_{k=1}^K$ and use augmented samples created by the generators to train the task model $F$.
comments.explanation: The paper uses $K$ both as the index for generators ($G_k$) and as a measure of training progress (e.g., mid-training OOD evaluation). This creates ambiguity: if OOD performance is measured after $K > 5$, is $K$ a generator index or a temporal step? The dual role confuses the evaluation protocol and risks misinterpretation. Reproducibility depends on clarifying whether $K$ tracks generator updates or training epochs.

comments.title: Potential Misattribution of Barlow Twins Loss Formulation
comments.quote: where _M_ refers to the cross-correlation matrix of the two positive-pair feature representations _z_ , _z_[+] , and _λ_ a user-set parameter.[3] BT (Eq. 4) is a combination of two terms balanced via a hyperparameter _λ_ , where the first term[] _i_[(1] _[ −][M][ii]_[)][2][ aligns two representations by spurring the diagonal values in] _[ M]_ of ( _z, z_[+] ) to be 1 while the second term[] _i_  _j_ = _i[M]_[ 2] _ij_[minimizes redundancy in the representation] by encouraging the off-diagonal values to be closer to 0.
comments.explanation: The text describes Barlow Twins as using a hyperparameter $\lambda$ to balance invariance and redundancy terms, but the standard formulation (Zbontar et al., 2021) uses a fixed $\beta$ rather than a tunable hyperparameter. While some variants do explore balancing weights, the paper fails to clarify that this is a deviation from standard BT. This risks misrepresenting the original method and misleads readers into thinking tuning $\lambda$ is standard practice.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Oracle Architecture Across Datasets
comments.quote: For Digits, we followed the practice of Cha et al. (2022) and used a true oracle pretrained on both the source and target domains of Digits.
comments.explanation: For Digits, the oracle uses target domain labels during training, unlike other datasets where it is trained only on source data and frozen. This violates the sDG assumption of no target access. The paper does not clarify how this affects fairness in comparison, risking invalid generalization. The claimed benefits of PROF might stem partly from privileged oracle knowledge, not just regularization. This distinction undermines interpretability and validity.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Backbone Architectures in Baseline Comparisons
comments.quote: For PACS, we adopted AlexNet... For Office-Home, we used a ResNet18...
comments.explanation: The paper draws architecture-dependent conclusions (e.g., ResNet18 benefits more than AlexNet) but uses different architectures on different datasets. Without within-dataset evaluation of both backbones, the claim is confounded by dataset differences. Extrapolating architectural effects across benchmarks is logically flawed and under-substantiated.

comments.title: Undefined Symbol in Loss Combination
comments.quote: where _w_ PROF and _w_ MDAR is a user-set parameter to activate two different methods, the oracle regularization PROF and our baseline MDAR.
comments.explanation: The text introduces _w_PROF_ and _w_MDAR_ as user-set parameters but does not link them to any equation or specify whether they are binary switches or continuous scalars. The grammatical singularity and lack of formal definition in an equation leave the loss combination underspecified. While conventional, reproducibility requires explicit exposition.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of Oracle model training in text vs. notation
comments.quote: The oracle model _O_ = _Co ◦ Ho_ consists of a frozen feature-extractor _Ho_ : _X →H_ and a trainable classification head _Co_ : _H →Y_.
comments.explanation: The notation labels _Co_ as 'trainable', yet the experiment section states the entire oracle is 'frozen' after finetuning. This creates a contradiction unless clarified that _Co_ is trained only during oracle setup and not during task model training. Without such temporal nuance, it misleads readers into thinking _Co_ is updated concurrently, undermining the notion of a stable oracle.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'wg = 1' then 'wg = 0' for generator training control
comments.quote: Generally, _wg_ = 1 during the first half of the training epochs for _Gk_ , then _wg_ = 0 to stop the training.
comments.explanation: The role of _wg_ is ambiguous because it is never linked to any loss equation in the provided text. While it likely scales the generator loss _Lg_, the absence of any formula involving _wg_ makes the mechanism underspecified. This disconnect between textual description and mathematical formulation risks confusion about how generator training is controlled.

comments.title: Figure 3 caption contradicts axis label
comments.quote:
**----- Start of picture text -----**

C-M C-P
